coredata.dc:title: Double emission fluorescence probes based on unconventional fluorescent molecules and fluorescein isothiocyanate for ClO− and Cu2+ detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, CAS. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, CAS. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: WANG, Ke-Xin
NIE, Chen
GUAN, Rui-Fang
ZHANG, Hao
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, unconventional fluorescent molecules without conjugated rings have attracted widespread interest due to their excellent fluorescence properties, simple preparation process, good water solubility and excellent biocompatibility. Dual emission fluorescent probes are usually more accurate and sensitive, for eliminating the influence of environmental factors. This work provides a novel strategy to construct double emission fluorescent probes based on unconventional fluorescent molecules and fluorescein isothiocyanate. The aqueous solution of resulting double emission polymer dots (f-PDs) shows two fluorescence emission peaks centered at 440 nm and 510 nm under excitation at 363 nm. According to change of these two peaks after adding ions, Cu2+ and ClO− are judged quantitatively. Cu2+ or ClO− are also quench fluorescence of f-PDs linearly, as Cu2+ combines with polyethyleneimine parts of f-PDs to form complexes, while ClO− oxidize hydroxyl groups on the surface of f-PDs. The detection limits of Cu2+ and ClO−, in an aqueous solution are 6.7 nM and 14.0 nM, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Double emission fluorescence probe
Polymer dots
Ion detection
Internal filtration effect
Fluorescein isothiocyanate

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of ClO− and Cu2+ based on f-PDs
The detection mechanism of Cu2+ by f-PDs
The quantitive fluorescence detection of Cu2+
The mechanism of detecting ClO− by f-PDs
The quantitive fluorescence detection of ClO−

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Scanning electron microscope image, (b) infrared spectrum and (c) nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectrum of f-PDs.
(a) XPS spectra of f-PDs, and the high-resolution spectra of (b) C1s, (c) N1s, and (d) O1s.

(a) Excitation, emission, and absorption spectra of f-PDs. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of f-PDs at different excitation wavelengths (290–410 nm). Variation of fluorescence intensity of f-PDs at 440 nm and 510 nm with (c) NaCl concentration, (d) UV lamp (365 nm) light time.

(a) Emission spectra before and after the addition of Cu2+ to f-PDs solutions, and (b) absorption spectra of f-PDs before and after the addition of Cu2+; (c) NMR spectra before and after the addition of Cu2+ to the f-PDs.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of f-PDs after the addition of different concentrations (0–50.0 μM) of Cu2+ . (b) Relationship between the ratio of the difference of fluorescence intensity at 440 nm and 510 nm before and after adding Cu2+ to f-PDs (I 440–I 510)/(I 440–I 510)0 and Cu concentration (0–50.0 μM). (c) Linearity of (I 440–I 510)/(I 440–I 510)0 with Cu2+ concentration in the range of 0–20.0 μM. (d) After adding 50.0 μM different cations (black bars), then adding 50.0 μM Cu2+, the fluorescence response (I 440–I 510)/(I 440–I 510)0 of f-PDs (red bars).
(From left to right) Optical photos of f-PDs and f-PDs under fluorescent and UV lamps (365 nm) after the addition of Ni3+, Sn2+, K+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Na+, Ag+, Fe3+.
(a) Fluorescence spectrum of f-PDs before and after ClO− addition. (b) Absorption spectra of f-PDs before and after ClO− addition. (c) NMR spectra before and after the addition of ClO− to the f-PDs.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of f-PDs after the addition of different concentrations (0–50.0 μM) of ClO− . (b) The relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio of f-PDs at 440 nm and 510 nm (I 440/I 510) and the concentration of ClO− (0–50.0 μM). (c) The linear relationship with I 440/I 510 (0–30.0 μM). (d) The fluorescence response (I 440/I 510) of f-PDs after adding 50.0 μM of different anions (black bars), and then adding 50.0 μM ClO− (red bars).

(From left to right) Optical photos of f-PDs and f-PDs under fluorescent and UV lamps (365 nm) after the addition of ClO−, SCN−, HSO3 −, F−, I−, SO4 2−, HPO4 −, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, H2O2, H2PO3 −, NO2 −, Na+, K+.
Preparation of f-PDs based on unconjugated fluorescent molecules for Cu2+ and ClO− detection.

head.ce:title: Double emission fluorescence probes based on unconventional fluorescent molecules and fluorescein isothiocyanate for ClO− and Cu2+ detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan, Shandong, 250022, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, unconventional fluorescent molecules without conjugated rings have attracted widespread interest due to their excellent fluorescence properties, simple preparation process, good water solubility and excellent biocompatibility. Dual emission fluorescent probes are usually more accurate and sensitive, for eliminating the influence of environmental factors. This work provides a novel strategy to construct double emission fluorescent probes based on unconventional fluorescent molecules and fluorescein isothiocyanate. The aqueous solution of resulting double emission polymer dots (f-PDs) shows two fluorescence emission peaks centered at 440 nm and 510 nm under excitation at 363 nm. According to change of these two peaks after adding ions, Cu2+ and ClO− are judged quantitatively. Cu2+ or ClO− are also quench fluorescence of f-PDs linearly, as Cu2+ combines with polyethyleneimine parts of f-PDs to form complexes, while ClO− oxidize hydroxyl groups on the surface of f-PDs. The detection limits of Cu2+ and ClO−, in an aqueous solution are 6.7 nM and 14.0 nM, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the intensity ratio of the two emission peaks is independent of the fluctuation of experimental conditions, double emission fluorescence probes are usually more sensitive. Several double emission fluorescence probes based on small organic molecules and quantum dots are reported [1–3]. The probes from small organic molecules are complex to design, difficult to synthesize and have low water solubility. And wide applications of quantum dots are limited because of their high cytotoxicity and low biocompatibility [3–5]. Unconjugated polymer dots composed of unconventional fluorescent molecules are easy to synthesize and widely used in cell imaging, ion probes, drug delivery and luminescent devices [6–11] because of their excellent photostability and water solubility, low toxicity and good biocompatibility (compared to quantum dots) [5,8,12]. In particular, they do not contain conjugated structure and typical luminescent groups, emitting strong fluorescence under suitable conditions has attracted the attention of many researchers. So far, although a great deal of research has been carried out in this area, but the published reports do not have a unified and in-depth explanation of the luminescence mechanism. Zhang et al. [13] prepared an unconjugated polymer dots, attributed its luminescence to molecular cross-linking agglomeration, oxidation-enhanced fluorescence and electron transfer between hydroxyl groups and chromophore. Guan et al. [14] prepared malonyl dihydrazide, which emits strong fluorescence in both solid state and solvent. The conclusion is that by forming inter/intra molecular hydrogen bonds, two or more planes containing C=O or C=N double-bond structures are constructed to be the fluorescence-emitting centers. In summary, the inability to clearly explain the emission mechanism of these unconventional fluorescent molecules has limited its preparation, application and further development. Therefore, constructing more various molecules is benefit to understand these unconventional fluorescent molecules further.

Hypochlorite (ClO−) is a relatively safe oxidant [15], which has been widely used in drinking water disinfection, bleaching, deodorization and so on [16–18]. However, excessive ClO− are carcinogenic and harmful to biology and the environment [19], such as trihalomethanes and dichloroacetonitrile. The World Health Organization (WHO) proposes that the Free Chlorine Residual in drinking water should be less than 0.2 mg L−1 [20], then detection of hypochlorite in drinking water is essential. Cu2+ is a necessary trace element for different and important metabolic functions in human body [21]. Deficiency of Cu2+ may lead to anemia, arteriosclerosis, osteoporosis, and other symptoms [22,23]. An excess amount of Cu2+ in the human body promotes production of reactive oxygen species, causing neurodegenerative disorder like acute copper poisoning, intrahepatic cholestasis in children, Alzheimer's disease and so on [24–28]. The WHO recommended that the maximum permissible amount of Cu2+ in drinking water is around 16.0 μM [29].

In this paper, a novel strategy is provided that double emission fluorescence probes are constructed based on unconjugated polymer dots and fluorescein isothiocyanate. Citric acid, polyethyleneimine and fluorescein isothiocyanate are treated hydrothermally, When the resulting double emission fluorescence probes (f-PDs) are excited by 363 nm, blue (440 nm) and yellow-green (510 nm) emission bands appear, the two peaks are corresponded to amide groups in unconjugated polymer dots and fluorescein isothiocyanate separately. f-PDs have excellent water solubility and high sensitivity, and are suitable for the detection of Cu2+ and ClO− in real water samples.

The citric acid (CA), polyethyleneimine (PEI, Mw=600), fluorescein isothiocyanate and inorganic salts were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Deionized water was used in all experiments. All chemicals used in this work are of analytical grade and without further purification.

The f-PDs were prepared by the hydrothermal method (Scheme 1 ). In short, 0.7 g citric acid, 0.5 g polyethyleneimine and 15 mg fluorescein isothiocyanate were evenly dispersed in 50 mL deionized water and stirred uniformly for 10 minutes, then heated to 120°C for 6 hours. Excessive absolute ethyl alcohol was used to wash the product precipitate for three times. Finally, f-PDs were dried to obtain the yellowish powder (purified f-PDs).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of ClO− and Cu2+ based on f-PDs
ce:section.ce:para: All fluorescence and absorption tests are carried out at room temperature. The pH of f-PDs dissolved in deionized water is about 5.0, and pH 5.0 was set for subsequent tests. The hydrochloride (except Ag+, which is AgNO3) and sodium salts were used to prepare various anion and cation solutions with a concentration of 0.01 mol L−1.

Ion-selective detection: The concentration of aqueous f-PDs solution is set as 1.0 μg mL−1. Various ion solutions were added to 2.0 mL of f-PDs solution at a time, with a final ion concentration of 50.0 M. The fluorescence emission spectra of f-PDs were detected in the presence of various ions (excitation wavelength: 363 nm, emission gap width: 5 nm).
Ion sensitivity test: 2.0 mL f-PDs solution (1.0 μg mL−1) was used to record the fluorescence emission spectrum with increasing ClO− and Cu2+ (0-50.0 μM) concentration.
In order to study the applicability of f-PDs in actual samples, tap water from laboratory was taken for environmental water sample analysis. Tap water is directly used without any treatment.

Citric acid, polyethyleneimine and fluorescein isothiocyanate were used as raw materials in this work. Under hydrothermal conditions, amino groups in polyethyleneimine react with carboxyl groups in citric acid and isothiocyanate groups in fluorescein isothiocyanate to form f-PDs.

As shown in Fig. 1 a, the scanning electron microscope (SEM) picture of f-PDs presents a sheet-like structure with a length of about 80 nm and a width of about 20 nm. Fourier transformed infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) is used to determine the type of functional group on f-PDs. As shown in Fig. 1b, there are two characteristic peaks of the amide group at 1575 cm−1 and 1612 cm−1, which proves that citric acid and polyethyleneimine are connected by amidation reactions. The characteristic peak of fluorescein isothiocyanate (−SCN) is at 2104 cm−1, and the disappearance of the characteristic peak in the black line proves the successful introduction of fluorescein isothiocyanate. In Fig. 1c, the proton peaks at 2.54 ppm and 2.67 ppm in the f-PDs originate from polyethyleneimine, while the peaks located at 1.13 ppm and 3.59 ppm are caused by residual anhydrous ethanol. The proton peak at 2.81 ppm in the high field region and the proton peak at 3.08–3.39 ppm in the low field region were derived from the methylene group of citric acid. The absence of peaks on fluorescein isothiocyanate in the spectra may be due to the levels being too low to be detected by the instrument. As shown in the full survey X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of f-PDs (Fig. 2 a), three main elements of C, N and O on the surface of f-PDs are identified. The high resolution C1s spectra (Fig. 2b) revealed four different carbon bond states: C−C (284.13 eV), C−N/C−O (285.28 eV), N−C=O (287.13 eV) and −COOH (289.28 eV). The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) mainly has two peaks, corresponding to N−C=O and −NH2 at 398.23 eV and 400.28 eV. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) mainly has three peaks at 530.08 eV, 531.18 eV and 531.73 eV, corresponding to N−C=O, −OH and −COOH. Three high-resolution spectra prove the existence of amide groups in f-PDs, which was consistent with the FTIR spectra. At the same time, hydrophilic groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups in f-PDs make them soluble.

The black line in Fig. 3 a is the absorption spectrum of f-PDs. The wide peak at 360 nm can be attributed to the n-π * transition caused by the agglomeration of amino, carboxyl and hydroxyl groups [30], and the peak at 483 nm is the characteristic peak of fluorescein isothiocyanate. From the red line in Fig. 2 (excitation spectrum of f-PDs), the optimal excitation peak is located in 363 nm, and under 363 nm, the double emission spectrum is obtained. The previous study found that the emission peak of nitrogen-containing nonconjugated polymer dots (NPDs) synthesized by citric acid and polyethyleneimine is about 440 nm [13]. Therefore, it can be speculated that the emission peak at 440 nm belongs to NPDs, and the emission peak at 510 nm belongs to fluorescein isothiocyanate. In addition, there is a large overlap between the emission part of f-PDs and the absorption part of fluorescein isothiocyanate in Fig. 3a, so an internal filtration effect (IFE) [31,32] occurs, and fluorescence intensity decreases. In Fig. 3b, the position of the emission peaks of the f-PDs at 442 nm and 510 nm do not change when the excitation wavelength is from 290 nm to 410 nm. Furthermore, f-PDs have high photostability (Fig. 3c). Even after 12 hours of continuous irradiation under ultraviolet (UV) lamp (365 nm), the fluorescence intensity at 440 nm decreased less than 10%, and the fluorescence intensity at 510 nm remained unchanged. Before the addition of fluorescein isothiocyanate, the fluorescence quantum yield measurements (quinine sulfate in 0.5 mol/L H2SO4 as the standard) was 53%, whereas after the addition of fluorescein isothiocyanate, the quantum yield of f-PDs was 47%. The decrease in quantum yield of f-PDs may be due to the an internal filtration effect between f-PDs and fluorescein isothiocyanate, but the quantum yields of f-PDs were still greater than those previously reported for fluorescent carbon dots (citric acid and polyethyleneimine as precursors), with quantum yields of 42.5% and 5.3%, respectively [31,32]. In addition, the variation of fluorescence intensity of f-PDs with ion concentration was explored (Fig. 3d). When the NaCl concentration reached 0.2 M, the fluorescence intensity of f-PDs at 440 nm and 510 nm remained essentially unchanged, indicating the excellent stability of f-PDs.

It has been reported that the amide bond network and functional groups (C=O,C=N,NH2) have significant effects on the production of blue fluorescence [33,34]. These functional groups containing lone pair electrons are partially cross-linked and produce new energy levels, resulting in radiative transitions. Referring to the previous achievements of our research group [13], N-PDs was synthesized from citric acid and polyethyleneimine under the same conditions. The blue fluorescence emission of N-PDs is attributed to the aggregation of functional groups containing lone pairs of electrons, which is the non-conjugated fluorescent part of this work.
The yellow-green emission of f-PDs is at 510 nm, which is close to the original fluorescence emission wavelength of fluorescein isothiocyanate (520–530 nm).
In summary, the blue emission of f-PDs comes from non-conjugated fluorescent part, and the yellow-green emission comes from fluorescein isothiocyanate.

f-PDs respond excellently to Cu2+ and ClO−. pH values selected to detect ions were first studied. Fig. S1a shows values of the fluorescence intensity of 440 nm (N-PDs) remain high level in the range of pH 2.0–7.0 and reach the highest point when pH is 5.0. At the same time, the fluorescence intensity of 510 nm (fluorescein isothiocyanate) disappeared when pH is lower than 4.0. That is, f-PDs is double emission probe from pH 5.0 to 13.0. In this paper, the pH value is set to 5.0 in subsequent experiments.

From Fig. S1b and S1c, the ion incubation time also has an effect on the fluorescence detection of f-PDs. After the addition of 50.0 μM ion solution, the fluorescence intensity decreased sharply within 1 minute and reached stable after 2 minutes. Then the pre-culture time of the experiment was set to 2 minutes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection mechanism of Cu2+ by f-PDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 a illustrates that the fluorescence peaks at both 440 nm and 510 nm of f-PDs were almost completely quenched (red line) after adding Cu2+. The mechanism of quenching effect of Cu2+ on f-PDs’ fluorescence is studied. In Fig. 4b, the absorption peaks of f-PDs after adding Cu2+ (red line) disappear at 360 nm, weakened at 483 nm, and a new absorption peak appears at 270 nm as well as 630 nm. This phenomenon is confirmed by Previous reports [13,35,36], that is, Cu2+ combines with the polyethyleneimine part of the fluorescent materials to form copper amine complexes. In addition, further evidence was provided using NMR spectroscopy and experimental phenomena. The addition of Cu2+ to the D2O solution of f-PDs resulted in the complete disappearance of the proton peak belonging to the polyethyleneimine (Fig. 4c), which further illustrates the above conclusion. Though Cu2+ had no quenching effect on the fluorescence of fluorescein isothiocyanate (Fig. S3), inserted picture in Fig. 4b demonstrates the overlap of absorption peaks of 360 nm and 483 nm, IFE occurs and further quenching dual fluorescence peaks of f-PDs at the same time. From the fluorescence lifetime graph (Fig. S5a), the fluorescence lifetime of f-PDs at 440 nm decreased from 3.92 ns to 1.29 ns after the addition of 50 μM Cu2+. In summary, the response of the f-PDs to Cu2+ is a combination of static quenching and dynamic quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The quantitive fluorescence detection of Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: The sensitivity and selectivity of f-PDs in detecting Cu2+ were evaluated (see Fig. 5 ). First of all, Fig. 5a shows that the fluorescence intensity of f-PDs at 440 nm and 510 nm decreases with increasement of Cu2+ concentration. Fig. 5b shows the relationship between the ratio of fluorescence intensity difference at 440 nm and 510 nm of f-PDs (I 440–I 510)/(I 440–I 510)0 and the concentration of Cu2+. It is clear that as the Cu2+ concentration increases from 0 to 20.0 μM, a good linear relationship is obtained with I 440–I 510/(I 440–I 510)0(Y=0.9657–0.037 X, R 2=0.992) (Fig. 5c), the correlation coefficient is 0.992 and the detection limit can be as low as 6.7 nM based on a signal-to-noise ratio of 3, which is lower than other probes previously reported (Table S1).

The selectivity of f-PDs to Cu2+ was tested. The test results are showed that in Fig. 5d, under the same experimental conditions, other cations (Ni3+, Sn2+, K+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Na+, Ag+, Fe3+) have a negligible effect on the fluorescence intensity of f-PDs, which is also confirmed by the photographs in Fig. 6 . And in the presence of other cations, Cu2+ detection can also be carried out. The above data show that f-PDs is an excellent fluorescence probe for Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of detecting ClO− by f-PDs
ce:section.ce:para: After adding ClO−, the fluorescence intensity of f-PDs at 440 nm decreased by about 75%, while that at 510 nm decreased by only about 25% (Fig. 7 a). Figure S2 illustrates that of f-PDs solution emits yellow-green fluorescence with addition of ClO− and the blue fluorescence part weakens.

As shown in Fig. 7b, the absorption peak (red line) belonging to f-PDs decreases obviously after addition of ClO−, indicating that the original static luminescence structure of f-PDs has been changed. This is similar to the luminescence of carbon dots related to their surface states [37–39]. In the NMR spectrum of the f-PDs after the addition of ClO− (Fig. 7c, the top), compared to the non-addition spectrum (Fig. 7c, the below), the proton peaks belonging to the citric acid fraction decrease after binding to ClO−. Therefore, it can be speculated that carboxyl groups on the surface of the f-PDs that reacts with ClO− [13], resulting in fluorescence quenching. ClO− was found to enhance the fluorescence of isothiocyanate fluorescein (Fig. S4). Because fluorescein isothiocyanate is slightly soluble in water, but more soluble in alkaline solution, with the alkalinity of the solution increases with the addition of NaClO, the fluorescence intensity is enhanced. Then the decrease of fluorescence intensity at 510 nm may be due to the IFE between blue part and fluorescein isothiocyanate after fluorescence quenching. With the fluorescence quenching of the blue part, the IFE gradually weakened, and the fluorescence peak of the yellow-green part gradually exceeded that of the blue part. At the same time, the fluorescence lifetime at 440 nm is reduced from 3.92 ns to 2.04 ns (Fig. S5b). To sum up, the response of f-PDs to ClO− is a combination of static quenching and dynamic quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The quantitive fluorescence detection of ClO−
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8 b and 8c show the relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio of f-PDs at 440 nm and 510 nm (I 440/I 510) and the concentration of ClO− (in the range of 0–30.0 μM). There is an excellent linear relationship with I 440/I 510 (Y=2.3048–0.06436 X, R 2=0.992) (Fig. 8c), S/N ratio of 3 and the detection limit is 14.0 nM, which is higher than most of the fluorescent probes (Table S3).

The selectivity and anti-interference ability of f-PDs to ClO− were tested, and the results are shown in Fig. 8d. Under the same experimental conditions, other anions and cations (SCN−, HSO3 −, F−, I−, SO4 2−, HPO4 −, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, H2O2, H2PO3 −, NO2 −, Na+, K+) had essentially no effect on f−PDs, only ClO− could quench the fluorescence of f-PDs. Moreover, the blue fluorescence of f-PDs diminishes and emits yellow-green fluorescence under UV lamp after the addition of ClO−(Fig. 9 ). And in the presence of other anions and cations, ClO− detection can also be carried out, with excellent anti-interference ability. If Cu2+ and ClO− coexist in the solution, the influence of Cu2+ can be attenuated by adding EDTA and biothiol (Fig. S6).

The synthesized f-PDs were successfully used for Cu2+ and ClO− detection in tap water. Tables S2 and S4 show the actual test results by mixing different contents of Cu2+ and ClO− into tap water. From the three repeated experiments, the recovery rate of Cu2+ was between 93.1% and 99.6%, and the relative standard deviation was 1.6% and 3.2%. The recovery rate of ClO− is between 93% and 102.2%, and the relative standard deviation is between 2.6% and 3.5% [40]. Cu2+ can't be detected in tap water for less Cu2+ in water than the detection limit. The content of ClO− in tap water is 3.2 μM, and the detection limit of f-PDs is 14.0 nM, which is much lower than that of ClO− in tap water. The above results prove that f-PDs can detect Cu2+ and ClO− in real water samples.

In a word, the double emission fluorescence probe f-PDs based on unconjugated part and fluorescein isothiocyanate was successfully synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method. Under the excitation of 363 nm, f-PDs have both blue (440 nm) and yellowish-green (510 nm) emission bands. The blue emission band originates from unconjugated polymer dots and the yellow-green emission band is attributed to fluorescein isothiocyanate. Moreover, f-PDs can detect Cu2+ and ClO− in aqueous solution quickly and sensitively. The emission intensity of f-PDs varies linearly with the ion concentration, and the detection limits are 6.7 nM and 14.0 nM, respectively. f-PDs was successfully used to detect Cu2+ and ClO− in tap water. This method has reference value for the construction of double emission probe based on unconjugated polymer with good water solubility.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2015BL011).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.cjac.2021.12.002.
